reasoning: The current observation shows that when mass is 10 kg and acceleration is 2 m/s², the force applied is 20 N. I hypothesize that the force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. To test this, I will increase both mass and acceleration and observe if the force remains constant.

The current observation shows that increasing the mass from 10 kg to 20 kg while keeping the acceleration constant at 2 m/s² results in a force of 40 N, which is twice the original force. This supports my hypothesis that force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. To further validate this, I will now increase the acceleration and see how the force changes.

The current observation shows that increasing the acceleration from 2 m/s² to 3 m/s² while keeping the mass constant at 20 kg results in a force of 60 N, which is three times the original force. This further supports my hypothesis that force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. Now, I will decrease both mass and acceleration to check for consistency.

The current observation shows that when mass is 10 kg and acceleration is 3 m/s², the force applied is 30 N. This confirms my hypothesis that force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. To ensure consistency, I will now decrease both mass and acceleration and observe the force.

The current observation shows that when mass is 10 kg and acceleration is 2 m/s², the force applied is 20 N. I hypothesize that the force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration. To test this, I will decrease both mass and acceleration and observe if the force remains constant.

The current observation shows that when mass is 0 kg and acceleration is 2 m/s², the force applied is 0 N. This suggests that when mass is zero, regardless of acceleration, there is no force acting on the object. Given this, the equation should not include mass when acceleration is zero. My hypothesis is that the force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration, but since mass cannot be zero, the equation must account for this special case.
